summary:
The Battle of Issus (333 BC) was the first direct confrontation between Alexander the Great and the Persian King Darius III, fought in a narrow coastal plain in what is now southern Turkey near the Syrian border. Despite being numerically outnumbered on constricted terrain, Alexander defeated the Persian royal army and nearly captured Darius himself, who fled on horseback abandoning his mother, wife, and children to Alexander's custody.

Darius had gathered a large army and maneuvered to cut Alexander's supply lines from the rear — an audacious strategic move that caught Alexander marching southward. The battle was fought in the narrow plain between the mountains and the sea, which negated much of Persia's numerical advantage. Alexander's strategy was characteristically direct: he personally led the Companion cavalry in a charge that struck the Persian left flank where Darius was positioned. When the Persian line threatened to give way around the king, Darius fled. His abandonment of the field led to the Persian army's collapse.

The capture of Darius's family was a diplomatic windfall. Alexander treated them with courtesy and respect, refusing to humiliate them, which enhanced his reputation for magnanimity and complicated Darius's position. After Issus, Darius made peace overtures offering half his empire; Alexander famously replied that he needed no offer since he already held all of Persia. The battle made clear that the Persian Empire's days were numbered, though the final reckoning would come at [[event:1035|Battle of Gaugamela]] two years later.
